1: When Balaam saw that it pleased YHWH to bless Israel, he did not go, as at the other times, to look for omens, but set his face toward the wilderness. The change in Balaam's approach is significant: in the first two oracles he followed the divination procedure (went to bare heights, waited for YHWH to come to him); now he simply faces toward Israel directly. The abandonment of the procedural apparatus signals a shift — either deeper surrender to YHWH's word or, more likely, recognition that the procedure has been futile. The third oracle will be the most spectacular. God's most profound revelations often come when human technique is abandoned and the prophet simply receives.
2: The Spirit of God came upon him (vatahi alav ruach Elohim). The third oracle is explicitly Spirit-inspired, where the first two were word-placed (vayasem YHWH davar befi). The shift marks the escalation: the Spirit comes upon him as in full prophetic inspiration. First Peter 1:11 describes the prophetic Spirit as the Spirit of Christ: the prophets “searched and inquired carefully, inquiring what person or time the Spirit of Christ in them was indicating when he predicted the sufferings of Christ and the subsequent glories.” Balaam's Spirit-inspiration is an instance of the Spirit of Christ testifying to the Messiah before the incarnation — the third oracle will be his greatest Christological contribution.
3: “The oracle of Balaam the son of Beor, the oracle of the man whose eye is opened.” Neum Bilam beno Beor unem hagever setum haayin — the man of the opened eye (setum in the Hebrew meaning opened/unveiled, from a root of uncovering). This self-designation after the donkey episode is pointed: Balaam is the man whose eyes were opened by divine action (v31 of Numbers 22). Spiritual sight is not a natural capacity but a divine gift. The prayer of Ephesians 1:18 is for this opened eye: “having the eyes of your hearts enlightened, that you may know what is the hope to which he has called you.”
4: “The oracle of him who hears the words of God, who sees the vision of the Almighty, falling down with his eyes uncovered.” The posture of falling down with eyes uncovered is the posture of receiving — prostration before the divine presence, eyes open to the vision. Isaiah, Ezekiel, and Daniel all fall prostrate before divine visions (Isaiah 6:5; Ezekiel 1:28; Daniel 8:17). John falls as if dead before the risen Christ (Revelation 1:17). The posture is universal: genuine encounter with the divine produces prostration. Christ's response to John is equally consistent with all such encounters: “Fear not, I am the first and the last, and the living one.” The prostration is met by the lifted hand of the living Savior.
5: “How lovely are your tents, O Jacob, your encampments, O Israel!” The famous opening of the third oracle moves immediately from prophetic self-description to exultation over Israel's beauty. The tents of Israel — a nomadic people in the wilderness — are declared lovely. Beauty here is covenantal, not aesthetic: Israel is lovely because she is YHWH's. Paul applies similar language to the church, the new Israel: Christ “loved the church and gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word, so that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle” (Ephesians 5:25-27). The beauty of the church, like the beauty of Israel's tents, is the beauty of divine possession and cleansing.
6: “Like palm groves that stretch afar, like gardens beside a river, like aloes that the LORD has planted, like cedar trees beside the waters.” The imagery of abundant, watered vegetation — palms, gardens, aloes, cedars — evokes Eden and the land of promise. The wilderness encampment is seen prophetically as a watered garden. Revelation 22:1-2 returns to this imagery for the new creation: “the river of the water of life, bright as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb… the tree of life with its twelve kinds of fruit.” The garden-by-waters imagery that Balaam sees prophetically is the eschatological reality that Christ's redemption produces — the restoration of Eden in the new Jerusalem.
7: “Water shall flow from his buckets, and his seed shall be in many waters; his king shall be higher than Agag, and his kingdom shall be exalted.” Water flowing abundantly — prosperity from YHWH. The king who is higher than Agag: Agag king of Amalek is the type of earthly kingship (1 Sam 15); the king who is higher than Agag is the one Samuel told Saul to destroy, whose survival became the symbol of incomplete obedience. The messianic king surpasses all earthly kings in authority. “His kingdom shall be exalted” — not Saul's interrupted kingdom, not David's limited kingdom, but the eternal kingdom of the one who is both David's son and David's Lord (Matthew 22:42-45).
8: “God brings him out of Egypt and is for him like the horns of the wild ox; he shall eat up the nations, his adversaries, and shall break their bones in pieces and pierce them through with his arrows.” The exodus power applied now to military conquest — the same God who delivered Israel from Egypt will give Israel victory over the nations. The imagery of bone-breaking (yigarem geramot) and piercing arrows recalls Psalms 2:9: “You shall break them with a rod of iron and dash them in pieces like a potter's vessel.” Psalm 2 is the messianic psalm that frames the entire Balaam narrative; the nations are broken not before Israel per se but before the Anointed One who fights for Israel. The wild ox horns of irresistible divine power find their final expression in the omnipotent Christ.
9: “He crouched, he lay down like a lion and like a lioness; who will rouse him up? Blessed are those who bless you, and cursed are those who curse you.” The oracle ends with the verbatim Abrahamic blessing-and-curse formula from Genesis 12:3: “I will bless those who bless you and him who dishonors you I will curse.” The Balaam narrative is from beginning to end a demonstration of this formula's reliability: Balak tried to curse; the attempted curse rebounded as blessing; the blessing-formula stands inviolate. Galatians 3:13-14 is the christological resolution: “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us… so that in Christ Jesus the blessing of Abraham might come to the Gentiles.” The one who became the curse has made the blessing available to all nations.
10: Balak's fury: “I called you to curse my enemies and behold, you have blessed them these three times.” Three oracles of blessing where three curses were commissioned. The number three is complete: Balak has exhausted every reasonable attempt. Rage before the sovereign purpose of God is the response of those who cannot accept that divine determination is not subject to human management. Herod's slaughter of the innocents (Matthew 2:16), the Sanhedrin's escalating pressure from arrest to trial to execution, Saul's persecution of the church (Acts 9:1-2) — all are forms of Balak's fury, the rage of those who will not accept that what God has blessed cannot be cursed.
11: “Flee to your own place. I said I would greatly honor you, but the LORD has kept you back from honor.” Balak dismisses Balaam empty-handed. The honor that was offered (kavod) as payment is withheld. Balaam departs without his fee. This is the cost of prophetic integrity maintained under pressure — or in Balaam's case, the cost of prophetic constraint enforced by God over his resistant prophet. The NT prophetic norm is that ministers of the word do not do it for “shameful gain” (1 Peter 5:2; Titus 1:7); the willingness to prophesy without honor is the test of genuine calling versus mercenary prophecy. Balaam accidentally demonstrates what he was constitutionally unable to freely choose.
12: Balaam's defense: “Did I not tell your messengers whom you sent to me, 'If Balak should give me his house full of silver and gold, I could not go beyond the word of the LORD to do either good or bad of my own will'?” The lo uchal la'avor et pi YHWH of 22:18 is quoted back verbatim. Balaam cites his own earlier statement as the definitive explanation. His integrity in this matter is real — he truly could not override the divine word. The paradox: he was genuinely constrained from cursing Israel, and genuinely counseled Balak on how to seduce Israel into self-destruction (Numbers 31:16). He found the gap between explicit prohibition and implicit permission. The enemy is never without tactics.
13: “What the LORD speaks, that will I speak.” Balaam's final defense is also his parting declaration. After all the attempts to purchase or maneuver a curse, he closes with the same principle that opened his involvement: I speak only what YHWH speaks. It is a true principle applied to a compromised character. The principle itself is not compromised by Balaam's flaws — it remains the foundation of all true prophecy. Christ is the one in whom the principle and the character are finally unified: he speaks only what the Father speaks, and his character is such that no gap exists between the principle and its application. There is no “teaching of Balaam” in Christ — no hidden counsel that works around the word he proclaims.
14: “And now, behold, I am going to my people. Come, I will let you know what this people will do to your people in the latter days.” Before departing, Balaam offers unsolicited prophecy about the distant future (be'acharit hayamim — in the latter days, the technical phrase for eschatological time). The “latter days” phrase situates what follows in the final age, beyond immediate history. The NT uses the same phrase for the messianic age inaugurated by Christ: “in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son” (Hebrews 1:2). Balaam's fourth and fifth oracles are thus eschatological — they point not to the immediately following history but to the final age, making them properly messianic prophecy.
15: The fourth oracle opens with the same self-description as the third (vv3-4), word for word: “the oracle of Balaam son of Beor, the oracle of the man whose eye is opened, who hears the words of God and knows the knowledge of the Most High, who sees the vision of the Almighty.” The repetition of the prophetic credential emphasizes: this word is authoritative. The fourth oracle will be the most directly messianic of all. The credential-before-oracle pattern is the structure of canonical prophecy: Isaiah's vision (Isa 6) precedes his proclamations; Ezekiel's throne-chariot vision (Ezek 1-3) precedes his commissions. Authority derives from the divine encounter, not from the prophet's social standing or education.
16: “Who hears the words of God, and knows the knowledge of the Most High.” The phrase “knowledge of the Most High” (daat Elyon) is unique to this passage. Balaam knows the knowledge of El Elyon — the God of Abraham (Genesis 14:19). The Most High is the universal divine name that transcends tribal deity — the God recognized even by Melchizedek. This connects the Balaam oracles to the Abrahamic covenant mediated by Melchizedek and ultimately to the Melchizedekian priesthood of Christ (Hebrews 7:1-17). The one who knows the knowledge of the Most High is the type of Christ who is himself the fullness of divine knowledge embodied: “in him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” (Colossians 2:3).
17: “I see him, but not now; I behold him, but not near: a star shall come out of Jacob, and a scepter shall rise out of Israel; it shall crush the forehead of Moab and break down all the sons of Sheth.” The Star Oracle — one of the most significant messianic prophecies in the OT. Darach kochav miYa'akov veqam shebet miYisrael — a star will step forth from Jacob, a scepter will arise from Israel. “Not now, not near” explicitly situates this in the distant eschatological future. The Dead Sea Scrolls use this verse messianically (1QM Numbers 11:6-7; 4QTestimonia); the rabbinic tradition identifies “Bar Kokhba” (Son of the Star) from this verse. Jesus applies the title to himself: “I am the root and the descendant of David, the bright morning star” (Revelation 22:16). The Magi follow a star from the east (Matthew 2:2) — the gospel deliberately echoes Balaam's oracle, announcing that the star has risen. The scepter of royal authority was promised to Judah (Genesis 49:10: “the scepter shall not depart from Judah”) and fulfilled in the one who holds the scepter of righteousness (Hebrews 1:8).
18: “Edom shall be dispossessed; Seir also, his enemies, shall be dispossessed. Israel is doing valiantly.” The military scope of the Star's work extends to Edom. Edom (Esau's line) is the persistent antagonist of Israel throughout the OT — Obadiah's entire prophecy is against Edom; Amos 9:12 promises the “remnant of Edom” will be possessed in the day of restoration. Acts 15:17 cites this in the context of Gentile inclusion: the fallen tent of David restored so that “all the nations who are called by my name” may seek the Lord. Edom's dispossession is not a national judgment in isolation but part of the cosmic reclamation of all territory for the LORD's kingdom.
19: “And one from Jacob shall exercise dominion and destroy the survivors of cities!” The one who exercises dominion (veyerd miYa'akov — one shall rule down from Jacob) is the messianic king. Psalm 72's vision of the king whose dominion reaches “from sea to sea and from the River to the ends of the earth” (Psalms 72:8) is the elaboration of this verse; Revelation 11:15 is its fulfillment: “The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, and he shall reign forever and ever.” The one who destroys the survivors is the king of final justice, before whom every enemy is finally subdued.
20: The fifth oracle is against Amalek: “Amalek was the first among the nations, but its end is utter destruction.” Amalek attacked Israel from behind in the wilderness (Exodus 17:8-16), targeting the weak. YHWH declared perpetual war against Amalek (Exodus 17:16). Amalek as the enemy who attacks the weak and vulnerable is the type of all powers that oppose God's people by targeting their weakness. Christ defeats this enemy not by superior force but by becoming weak himself — the incarnation as the ultimate vulnerability that defeats the power that preys on weakness. “The weakness of God is stronger than men” (1 Corinthians 1:25).
21: The sixth oracle is against the Kenites: “Enduring is your dwelling place, and your nest is set in the rock.” Kenites are associated with Jethro, Moses's father-in-law (Judges 1:16), and with Hobab the wilderness guide of Numbers 10. They nestled among Israel, seemingly secure. Yet the oracle pronounces eventual Assyrian exile. Security in a rock is only as reliable as the rock. The NT redefines the true rock: “And the Rock was Christ” (1 Corinthians 10:4). Those who build their house on the rock will stand; those who build on any other foundation will fall (Matthew 7:24-27). The Kenites' earthly rock-dwelling foreshadows the contrast between earthly and heavenly security.
22: “Nevertheless, Kain shall be burned when Asshur takes you away captive.” The Assyrian exile of the northern kingdom is anticipated in Balaam's oracle — a prophecy from the plains of Moab about events six centuries in the future. This long time-horizon of prophecy is itself a theological statement: YHWH's knowledge and plan extend across centuries. Peter uses the same long-horizon argument to answer those who doubt the second coming: “With the Lord one day is as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day” (2 Peter 3:8). Divine prophecy operates outside human temporal frameworks; the long gap between oracle and fulfillment demonstrates sovereign foreknowledge, not delay.
23: “Alas, who shall live when God does this? Ships shall come from Kittim and shall afflict Asshur and Eber; and he also shall come to utter destruction.” The question “who shall live?” (mi yichyeh missumo El) is the question of survival before eschatological judgment. Revelation 6:17 asks the same question: “Who can stand?” — asked when the great day of wrath arrives. The answer in Revelation is the sealing of the 144,000 and the multitude clothed in white who have washed their robes in the blood of the Lamb (Revelation 7:9-14). The answer to “who shall live?” is: those in Christ, whose death is the shield against the final judgment.
24: The oracle ends: “and he also shall come to utter destruction.” The last word of Balaam's oracles is destruction — but it is the destruction of every enemy of God's people, not of God's people themselves. The trajectory of all four oracles: from the impossibility of cursing Israel, to the irrevocability of blessing, to the messianic star and scepter, to the final destruction of all who oppose God's king. This is the arc of Revelation: the attempt to curse the church (persecution), the irrevocability of the seal of God on his people, the advent of Christ the Morning Star, and the final destruction of every enemy. Balaam's oracles are a miniature apocalypse.
25: Balaam rises and returns to his place, and Balak goes his own way. They depart together into silence — no fulfillment, no curse, no blessing received by Balak, no fee collected by Balaam. The narrative ends with both principals disappointed. Yet the oracles stand, their fulfillment unaffected by the departure of the prophet who spoke them. This is the character of divine word: it stands after every human actor exits the stage. Isaiah 55:11: “my word that goes out from my mouth; it shall not return to me empty, but it shall accomplish that which I purpose.” Balaam departs, Balak is frustrated — and a Star shall come out of Jacob. The oracle cannot be cancelled by the departure of its speaker, any more than Caiaphas's prophecy could be cancelled by his ignorance of what he prophesied (John 11:51-52).
